segments.title: Gunboat diplomacy and shallow-draft force
segments.summary: The video opens by separating gunboat diplomacy from literal gunboats, then explains why shallow-draft armed vessels mattered for inland pressure.
segments.body: This watch point is useful for the basic definition problem: naval coercion could involve cruisers, destroyers, or battleships, while actual gunboats solved a different geography problem. Dr. Clarke moves from examples in Campbell's Gunboat Diplomacy to the practical need for a shallow-draft vessel with a meaningful gun when a navy wanted influence up rivers or close inshore. The segment frames the gunboat as a mobile command post, symbol of sovereignty, and fire-support platform rolled into one small hull.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces Campbell's gunboat diplomacy examples and notes that gunboat diplomacy is often carried out by ships that are not gunboats.
The transcript defines the shallow-draft, big-gun logic and describes gunboats as mobile command posts, status symbols, and direct or indirect fire platforms.

segments.title: Insect and Fly class river support model
segments.summary: The Insect and Fly classes show gunboats acting as littoral combat ships, river scouts, supply escorts, and troop fire-support craft.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses the First World War Insect and Fly gunboats to show why the type was more than diplomatic theater. In Iraq, the Army moved along the river while gunboats carried supplies, ranged ahead for reconnaissance, and gave fire support. The example shows why a river gunboat could combine logistics, scouting, and close support in places where larger warships or land transport could not do the same work.

evidence.note: The transcript connects Insect and Fly gunboats to littoral combat, brown-water operations, and the Iraq campaign.
The transcript describes river movement, supply carriage, reconnaissance by gunboat, fire support, and troops landing from barges.

segments.title: Yangtze Patrol and Panay crisis warning
segments.summary: USS Panay turns river gunboats into a lesson about the number of 1930s Far East incidents that could have widened into war.
segments.body: After using USS Panay as a Yangtze Patrol example, the lecture pauses on her sinking on 12 December 1937. The point is not only that gunboats protected interests and nationals in China, but that river patrols sat directly inside the region's crisis politics. The episode helps explain why British and American attention before 1939 could not be reduced to Europe alone.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces USS Panay as a Yangtze Patrol gunboat and notes that she was sunk by the Japanese on 12 December 1937.
The transcript argues that many Far East incidents in the 1930s could have led to war before the European war began.

segments.title: Dragonfly class design and Far East role
segments.summary: The Dragonfly class appears as a late-1930s Royal Navy answer to Far East presence needs rather than as a true armored warfighting class.
segments.body: This segment gives the Dragonfly-class entry point. Dr. Clarke lists the construction timing around Dragonfly, Grasshopper, Scorpion, Locust, and Mosquito, then ties the ships to Japanese and China-related pressures in the Far East. The important caveat is that he treats them as presence and river-work vessels, not armored combatants built to stand in a fleet fight.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the Dragonfly class and gives construction/completion details for Dragonfly, Grasshopper, Scorpion, Locust, and Mosquito.
The transcript says the ships were built amid Far East issues and were not armored warfighting vessels, with displacement details following.

segments.title: Dragonfly losses and the Far East investment gap
segments.summary: The wartime fate of the Dragonfly-class gunboats becomes a force-planning warning about Far East readiness and neglected small ships.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke notes that HMS Locust was the only vessel of the group to survive the Second World War, while the others were lost around Dunkirk or in the Far East. He then uses Dragonfly and Grasshopper's February 1942 loss to show the vulnerability of small presence craft once the regional crisis became open war. The larger argument is about peacetime underinvestment: Britain needed many low-end support vessels and worked-up Far East forces, but war in Europe pulled attention and capacity away.

evidence.note: The transcript says HMS Locust was the only survivor and that remaining ships were lost at Dunkirk or in the Far East in February 1942.
The transcript describes Dragonfly and Grasshopper sailing with troops for Sumatra and being hit by aircraft in the Banka Strait.
The transcript links the Far East losses to lack of investment in sloops, the European war, and insufficient worked-up Far East forces.

segments.title: Treaty-exempt small ships and missed procurement
segments.summary: A late treaty discussion argues that navies could have built useful small escorts and gunboats without treaty tonnage limits, but failed to prepare production.
segments.body: The lecture highlights the London Naval Treaty exemption for small vessels of about 2,000 tons and below, then asks why navies did not exploit it for escorts, gunboats, and anti-submarine craft. Dr. Clarke points to Fairmile development as proof that small craft could scale quickly in war, but argues the production base should have been nurtured before 1939. This is a procurement lesson: cheap small vessels were not glamorous, yet their absence left navies short of exactly the craft they needed in aggregate.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses small ships under treaty limits and names escort, torpedo, minelaying, and mine-sweeping roles as crucial small-vessel functions.
The transcript says a 2,000-ton sloop could be useful and criticizes maritime nations for not building more escorts and gunboats outside treaty limits.
The transcript uses the Fairmile progression and hypothetical annual orders to argue that peacetime production capacity would have mattered in wartime.

segments.title: Soviet river gunboats and American PGM mismatch
segments.summary: The final comparison contrasts Soviet armored river gunboats with American PGM craft whose speed made them poor companions for PT boat attacks.
segments.body: The Soviet section shows a navy that understood river fire support: BKA 1124 and 1125 craft used tank turrets, machine guns, and later rockets to support river crossings and operations on the Baltic, Black Sea, and inland waterways. The American section is sharper criticism. Dr. Clarke argues that PG-1/PGM and PGM-9 gunboats had useful weapons but not enough speed to support 40-knot PT boats through an attack and withdrawal, which helps explain why PT crews improvised extra firepower on their own boats.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces Soviet BKA 1124 and 1125 river gunboats, tank turrets, rockets, and operations in the Baltic, Black Sea, and rivers.
The transcript says the US Navy used S-497-class submarine chasers for PG-1/PGM support craft and notes their 21-knot speed problem against fast PT boats.
The transcript argues the speed mismatch forced PT boats to slow down, says PGM-9s were slower still, and links the gap to PT boat self-adaptation.
